attributes.stem:
A 42-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of persistent vulval itch and burning sensation. She reports noticing raised white patches on her vulva that have gradually increased in size. She has no vaginal discharge or bleeding. Her medical history includes systemic lupus erythematosus treated with prednisolone and azathioprine. She is a current smoker with a 20 pack-year history. She does not take any other regular medications and does not believe in vaccinations. She has refused previous cervical screening due to personal beliefs.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Vulval examination reveals raised, well-demarcated white and brown plaques on the labia majora with areas of erythema. The lesions are not tender. There is no lymphadenopathy.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with chronic vulval symptoms and examination findings suggestive of vulval intraepithelial neoplasia (VIN). The presence of well-demarcated white plaques, along with significant risk factors including immunosuppression and smoking, supports this diagnosis. It typically presents in patients in their 40s though may occur at any age.

Human papillomavirus (HPV) infection is also highly associated with VIN (types 16, 18 and 33) though regular cervical screening tests and immunisation against the HPV has decreased the development of VIN. Vulval intraepithelial neoplasia is a pre-malignant condition that requires biopsy for definitive diagnosis and careful follow-up due to the risk of progression to invasive cancer over 2- 7 years. Management after initial staging and biopsy typically involves surgical excision or laser ablation, along with smoking cessation and careful follow-up.

Lichen sclerosus typically presents with white or silver atrophic patches that have a characteristic 'cigarette paper' appearance and may cause architectural changes of the vulva. It may also present with mild itch or discomfort. While it can occur in this age group, the raised nature of the lesions and the patient's risk factors make VIN more likely.

Vulval candidiasis typically presents with cottage cheese-like discharge, intense itch, and erythema. The chronic nature of symptoms and the presence of raised white plaques make this diagnosis less likely.

Psoriasis of the vulva typically presents with well-demarcated erythematous plaques that may be scaly. While it can occur in immunosuppressed patients, the white appearance of the lesions is not typical for psoriasis.

Contact dermatitis usually presents with erythema, burning, and itch in response to an identifiable irritant or allergen. The chronic nature and appearance of the lesions are not consistent with this diagnosis.
learningPoints: Vulval intraepithelial neoplasia may be asymptomatic or presents with persistent vulval itch and visible lesions that may be white, red, or pigmented.
Risk factors for vulval intraepithelial neoplasia include HPV infection, smoking, and immunosuppression.
Biopsy is essential for definitive diagnosis of vulval intraepithelial neoplasia.
references: Ngan V, Oakley A, Rowan D.  Vulval intraepitherlial neoplasia.  DermNet NZ.  2003.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/vulval-intraepithelial-neoplasia
Kesić V, Vieira-Baptista P, Stockdale CK. Early Diagnostics of Vulvar Intraepithelial Neoplasia. Cancers (Basel). 2022 Apr 4;14(7).  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8997501/
